risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The women were randomised by SurveyMonkey, a web-based survey tool." and "All women meeting the inclusion criteria were randomly assigned to the treatment groups using the SurveyMonkey tool." Comment: The paper describes random assignment using a web-based computerized tool, which is a sufficient context clue for a proper random sequence generation method.

Quote: "The women were randomised by SurveyMonkey, a web-based survey tool." and "All women meeting the inclusion criteria were randomly assigned to the treatment groups using the SurveyMonkey tool. Four women were not internet users. They answered the questions by phone, and results were manually entered into SurveyMonkey by the first author (MS) before randomisation." Comment: The report states that a web-based tool was used for randomisation, but it does not explicitly describe a concealment mechanism showing that the person enrolling participants could not foresee the upcoming assignment. In particular, it does not say that allocation was centrally administered or inaccessible to the recruiter/investigator. Because sequence generation/randomisation is described but concealment from recruiters is not explicitly reported, allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A three-armed randomised controlled trial was conducted, comparing 12 sessions of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) acupuncture in one group, 12 h of PFMT in a second group... and a waiting list control group" and "The women randomised to this group received no treatment". Quote: "ICIQ-UI SF is a well validated self-reporting outcome form". Comment: The trial compared clearly different interventions, including a no-treatment waiting list, so participants and treating personnel would have known group assignment; no blinding of participants/personnel is described. Because the main outcomes were self-reported urinary incontinence symptoms (ICIQ-UI SF) and expectations were measured and discussed as related to outcomes, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence results. This is positive evidence of absent blinding with subjective outcomes, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "ICIQ-UI SF is a well validated self-reporting outcome form translated into 38 languages." and "This template included boxes for inclusion criteria, exercise diary, questions about expectation of treatment effect, adverse events, satisfaction with the treatment, and whether the women would recommend the treatment for others with MUI" and "The women randomised to this group received no treatment" Comment: The outcome assessor for the main clinical outcome and other reported outcomes was the patient self-reporting via questionnaire/SurveyMonkey. Participants were necessarily unblinded because one group received acupuncture, one PFMT, and one was a waiting-list/no-treatment control. These outcomes include subjective symptom and satisfaction measures that could readily be influenced by knowledge of allocation. By the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded self-reported subjective outcomes makes detection bias high risk.

Quote: "The number of dropouts was high in all groups: 50% in the waiting list group, 40% in the physiotherapy group, and 25% in the acupuncture group." and Table 1: "n ... Acupuncture 12 ... Physiotherapy 10 ... Waiting list 12." and Discussion: "Three of the four dropouts in the physiotherapy group dropped out before attending the first session... Another woman... dropped out after her first session... In the acupuncture group, only two women withdrew during the study, one due to no effect, and the other due to cancer. A further two dropped out before treatment, leaving eight women completing 12 sessions of acupuncture during 12 weeks." Comment: For the analysed 12-week ICIQ-UI SF outcome, post-randomization attrition was substantial and imbalanced across arms: about 3/12 (25%) acupuncture, 4/10 (40%) physiotherapy, and 6/12 (50%) waiting list, with a differential loss of 25 percentage points between arms and overall losses >20%. Reasons included potentially outcome-related withdrawal ("no effect") in the acupuncture arm. No robust missing-data method or ITT analysis with imputation is reported; results appear based on completers (e.g. waiting-list discussion refers to "the six women completing the forms"). This meets High-risk criteria for attrition bias.
